prompt:
Change windows on the fleet are currently a wiki page and crossed fingers, and the last bad window proved we cannot reconstruct afterwards what was applied where or what to unwind. I want changerun.ps1: a planner that turns our desired-state model plus per-host facts into an ordered change plan, then tracks every step through an explicit lifecycle as executor results come back, including rollback. The executor itself runs elsewhere and hands us its outcomes as JSON -- this tool owns the plan and the state, never the hosts. Acceptance: pwsh -NoProfile -File test_changerun.ps1 (that file and everything under fixtures/ are read-only). One entry point, four commands:

pwsh -NoProfile -File changerun.ps1 plan -Desired <file> -Facts <dir> -State <file>
pwsh -NoProfile -File changerun.ps1 apply -State <file> -Results <file>
pwsh -NoProfile -File changerun.ps1 rollback -State <file>
pwsh -NoProfile -File changerun.ps1 status -State <file>

Any other (or missing) command => stderr 'usage: changerun.ps1 plan|apply|rollback|status' and exit 64. Any input path that does not exist => stderr 'changerun: not found: <value as given>' and exit 66. Every hard error below goes to stderr with exit 65, stdout stays empty, and the state file is left exactly as it was (or not created).

The desired model is { "changes": [ { id, role, setting, value, requires: [ids] } ] }. Facts are one JSON per host in -Facts: { host, role, settings: { name: value } } (string values). plan: for each host whose role has changes, a change is needed when the host's setting is absent or differs (case-sensitive). Ordering is two-level: hosts in ordinal host-name order; within a host, dependency order over the role's FULL change graph -- Kahn's algorithm, always draining the ready set in ordinal id order. requires must name a change id of the same role: anything else => "changerun: unknown requirement '<req>' of '<id>'" (changes checked in listed order, requires in listed order); a cycle => 'changerun: requirement cycle detected'. A change that is already satisfied produces no step but still participates in ordering (a satisfied prerequisite does not drag its dependents out of order, and a host can need a dependent without needing the prerequisite). Each needed change becomes a step with id '<host>/<changeId>'. plan prints one line per step, 'planned <id> <setting>: <from> -> <to>' with (none) as <from> when the host lacks the setting, writes the state file, and exits 0 (empty plan: no output, state written with an empty step list).

The state file is the contract between runs -- JSON shaped exactly { "steps": [ { id, host, change, setting, from, to, status } ] } in plan order, from null when the setting was absent. Statuses are the four-state machine, and the ONLY legal transitions are this table:

  planned -> applied      (apply, executor outcome ok)
  planned -> failed       (apply, executor outcome fail)
  applied -> rolled-back  (rollback, on a host that has a failed step)

Everything else is refused with "changerun: invalid transition '<from>' -> '<to>' for step '<id>'". Any command loading a state file first validates every status and rejects unknowns with "changerun: unknown status '<status>' for step '<id>'" (first offending step in stored order).

apply reads -Results ({ "outcomes": { "<stepId>": "ok"|"fail" } }) and walks the steps in stored order. Before anything transitions it validates: every step must currently be planned (else the invalid-transition error above, first offender in order -- so re-applying an applied or rolled-back state is refused), and every step must have an outcome (else "changerun: no outcome for step '<id>'"). Then per step: outcome ok => applied, printed 'applied <id>'; outcome fail => failed, printed 'failed <id>'; once a step on some host has failed, every later step on THAT host is printed 'skipped <id>' and stays planned (other hosts are unaffected). Write the state back; exit 0 when nothing failed, 65 when at least one step failed (the runbook branches on that).

rollback unwinds hosts that have at least one failed step: on each such host every applied step transitions to rolled-back in REVERSE stored order, printed 'rolledback <id>' (hosts in ordinal order, reversal within the host). Hosts without failures keep their applied steps. Write the state back, exit 0. Running rollback again is a no-op (empty output, exit 0).

status prints exactly one line, 'planned=N applied=N failed=N rolled-back=N', counting steps, and exits 0.

Everything deterministic: ordinal comparisons and sorts only, no Get-Date anywhere, no Format-* output, LF endings. Set-StrictMode -Version Latest must survive the whole tool.
notes: 6P9 item 3: fleet-config planner capstone (the S1 state-machine sibling, PowerShell edition). State-machine core planned/applied/failed/rolled-back pinned by an explicit transition table: legal transitions exercised through the full lifecycle, illegal ones pinned via double-apply (applied->applied), apply-after-rollback (rolled-back->applied), and a doctored 'held' status fixture; persisted JSON state file is the pinned cross-run contract. Dependency-aware ordering: Kahn with ordinal ready set over each role's full change graph, with the satisfied-prerequisite case pinned (web02 needs vhost2 but not its prerequisite tls13). Rollback = reverse stored order on failed hosts only. PowerShell-native pins: ConvertFrom-Json object-graph traversal under StrictMode incl. null round-trip of 'from', hashtable-free property-bag walking, ordinal-only ordering. Differentiation: ps-svcplan (6P4-6) is a STATELESS one-shot service action planner (no persistence, no lifecycle, no results ingestion, no rollback) -- this seed's core is the persisted state machine across multiple invocations; go-orderfsm/py-fsmcore are in-memory runtime FSMs, not planners; py-sync-plan has no state file or rollback.
